name: Under Armour, Inc.

profile.description: Under Armour, Inc. designs, develops, markets, and distributes athletic and outdoor apparel, footwear, and accessories. Founded by Kevin Plank in 1996, the company is executing a multi-year transformation under Plank's returned leadership, having completed a $305M restructuring program that included exiting the Curry Brand, cleaning up off-price distribution channels, and refocusing on its core performance athletic identity. FY2026 revenue was $5.0B with a net loss of $496M including a $247M deferred tax valuation allowance and $128M in restructuring charges.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (0/3): Under Armour has not launched a successful new product, entered a new category, or executed a business model transformation in the last five years. The company is actively retreating — exiting the Curry Brand basketball line and winding down off-price wholesale channels [1][2]. The 'transformation' is brand repair, not innovation. AI Deployment (1/2): Under Armour operates MapMyFitness and UA Connected technology in footwear, and has invested in predictive supply-chain tools for demand forecasting and overstock reduction [3]. However, no AI-powered products are shipping to customers with measurable revenue impact. This earns 1 point for AI exploration without quantified results. Market Share (0/2): Under Armour's market share has declined to approximately 2.3% of the athletic apparel market in 2025, down from a peak near 5%, compared to Nike at 20.5% and Lululemon at 4.8% [3]. The company is losing share, not gaining it. Product Pipeline (0/2): FY2027 guidance calls for revenue 'down slightly' with no specific new product launches named. The Curry Brand exit removes a revenue stream without a replacement. No next-generation products have been announced with specific timelines. R&D Intensity (0/1): Under Armour's R&D spending as a percentage of revenue is below Nike and Adidas, with no evidence of top-quartile investment in product innovation [3].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Under Armour's mission — 'to make all athletes better through passion, design, and the relentless pursuit of innovation' — is specific, distinctive, and connects the brand to a purpose beyond profit. Kevin Plank's founding story (developing moisture-wicking fabric as a frustrated football player) gives the mission authentic roots [4]. However, the mission has not been consistently validated by decades of execution — the company drifted significantly under prior CEO Patrik Frisk into dilutive off-price distribution that contradicted the performance brand identity. Community Impact (1/2): Under Armour maintains partnerships with collegiate athletic programs and elite athletes, and Plank has emphasized an 'underdog' culture that resonates with aspiring athletes [4]. However, the company has no major distinctive foundation or community investment program with quantified impact comparable to peers. Employee Retention (0/2): Multiple waves of restructuring, three CEO transitions in four years (Plank to Frisk to Plank), and significant workforce reductions make it impossible to claim strong retention. No public data on voluntary turnover or average tenure. Insider Buying (1/2): A major shareholder purchased 739,521 shares of UAA in May 2026, and Kevin Plank maintains ownership of approximately 17.1 million shares worth over $100M [5]. Plank's continued significant equity ownership signals conviction, though the May 2026 purchase was by an external major shareholder rather than Plank himself on the open market.

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Liquidity check: $6.01 x 8,289,691 avg daily volume = $49.8M, comfortably above the $1M floor. FY2026 full-year revenue declined 4% to $5.0B with a net loss of $496M including $247M deferred tax valuation allowance and $128M in restructuring charges [1]. D/E of 137.1% scores 0.71 (above 70% target but well below 300% floor). ROE of -30.0% scores 0.0 (below the 0% floor). Revenue growth YoY of -0.8% scores 0.64. Revenue growth 5yr CAGR of approximately -3.0% scores 0.13. Forward P/E of 24.80 scores 0.88. Profit margin of -10.0% scores 0.0. PEG ratio unavailable, excluded. Weighted sum (with 2x weighting on ROE and 5yr CAGR for Growth stocks): 2.50 / 8.0 = 0.31 x 10 = 3.1, rounded to 3. The only bright spots are manageable debt levels and a forward P/E suggesting analysts expect profitability to recover. FY2027 guidance projects adjusted operating income of $140M-$160M and gross margin expansion of 220-270 basis points [2].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Under Armour's core business — designing, manufacturing, and distributing physical athletic apparel and footwear — involves tangible products, brand identity, athlete endorsements, and global supply chain management that AI cannot substitute [3]. Athletic wear requires physical fitting, tactile fabric innovation, and brand-driven purchase decisions that remain fundamentally human. The company is protected by the physical nature of its products and the emotional/aspirational dimension of sports branding. However, AI does create meaningful competitive pressure: Nike's 190M+ app users enable AI-driven personalization and demand forecasting at a scale Under Armour cannot match with its smaller data assets. Generative AI could accelerate competitors' design cycles and reduce time-to-market. Analysts have noted that retailers risk losing direct customer interaction as consumers increasingly use external AI shopping platforms [3]. The risk is competitive erosion from AI-enabled rivals, not direct business model substitution. AI Opportunity (0/1): Under Armour operates MapMyFitness and has invested in predictive supply-chain tools, but no quantifiable AI-specific revenue has been reported. The connected fitness platform has not been monetized as an AI product. Until the company reports specific AI revenue figures, this scores 0.

infinite_game.summary: Under Armour under Kevin Plank's returned leadership is attempting to restore infinite game thinking after years of finite-minded drift. The original Just Cause — making athletes better through performance innovation — is genuine and Plank's willingness to accept painful revenue contraction to rebuild brand equity shows some courage. However, the company has been in reactive restructuring mode for six years, trust has been eroded by leadership instability and workforce reductions, and it remains unclear whether the turnaround is driven by a restored Just Cause or by financial necessity. Sinek would see potential but demand proof of sustained purpose-driven execution before scoring higher.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Under Armour's founding purpose — making athletes better through performance innovation — is genuine and distinctive. Kevin Plank built the company from a single moisture-wicking shirt in his grandmother's basement, and the 'underdog athlete' identity gives the cause authentic emotional resonance [4]. Plank's return to the CEO role in April 2024 was explicitly framed as restoring this original purpose after years of drift under Patrik Frisk's operationally-focused leadership. However, the cause has not been validated by decades of consistent execution — the company spent 2019-2024 in strategic drift, over-expanded into off-price channels that diluted the performance brand, and is now restructuring to return to its roots. Sinek would credit the genuine cause but note that it has been compromised and must be re-proven. The $434M securities fraud settlement [6] further undermines the narrative of purpose-driven leadership continuity.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Under Armour's organizational environment scores at the floor of Sinek's framework. Three CEO transitions in four years (Plank to Frisk to Plank), a $305M restructuring program with $128M in charges in FY2026 alone, and significant workforce reductions from 17,500 to approximately 15,000 employees create an environment where psychological safety is difficult to maintain [1][4]. Plank has publicly stated he believes in micromanagement 'at certain levels,' which contradicts Sinek's emphasis on empowerment and trust [4]. The February 2026 senior leadership reorganization — while potentially beneficial long-term — represents yet another structural upheaval for teams to absorb. Former CFO David Bergman's departure and replacement adds further instability at the top.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Under Armour explicitly and constructively acknowledges Nike and Adidas as rivals whose strengths expose Under Armour's own weaknesses. Plank's return narrative is built around 'learning from what Nike did right' in brand storytelling, digital ecosystem development, and global distribution [3][4]. The competitive language is about self-improvement — Plank frames the challenge as 'getting back to who we are' rather than 'beating Nike.' The company's willingness to cite On Running and Hoka as emerging competitors who have captured mindshare shows healthy competitive awareness. Sinek would credit this mindset.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Under Armour has made meaningful strategic shifts — exiting the Curry Brand basketball line despite Stephen Curry's star power, winding down off-price wholesale distribution that generated revenue but diluted the brand, and executing a $305M restructuring to fundamentally reshape the cost structure [1][2]. The Curry Brand exit in particular required walking away from a marquee athlete partnership in service of a more focused brand identity. However, these moves are defensive retreats rather than proactive transformative pivots. The company is returning to its original identity, not reinventing itself for a new market reality. Sinek would distinguish between flexibility born of necessity (Under Armour) and flexibility born of vision (Apple abandoning the iPod for the iPhone).

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Plank's willingness to accept consecutive years of revenue decline (FY2025 -9.4%, FY2026 -4%, FY2027 guided 'down slightly') in service of rebuilding brand equity demonstrates genuine short-term sacrifice for long-term positioning [1][2]. The decision to exit the Curry Brand, accept a $434M securities fraud settlement [6], and absorb $305M in restructuring charges while guiding to only $140M-$160M in adjusted operating income for FY2027 shows leadership willing to endure pain. However, it is difficult to disentangle courage from necessity — the company had little choice but to restructure after years of declining performance. Sinek would credit one point for the willingness to endure revenue contraction, but would want to see proactive investment (not just cost-cutting) to award full marks.

investment_thesis: Under Armour's bull case rests on a founder-led turnaround entering its inflection phase: Kevin Plank's return has stabilized the brand narrative around core performance athletics, gross margin is guided to expand 220-270 basis points in FY2027 (to approximately 47.7-48.2%), and adjusted operating income of $140M-$160M would mark the first meaningful profitability since the restructuring began [1][2]. At $6.01/share with a $2.7B market cap, the stock trades near tangible book value with significant option value if North American revenue stabilizes. EMEA grew 9% in FY2026 and is guided for low-single-digit growth in FY2027, providing an international growth offset while the core North American business heals. The $305M restructuring program is substantially complete, meaning the company is transitioning from cost-cutting to growth investment, with $30M in incremental marketing spend planned for FY2027 [2]. A major shareholder increased their stake in May 2026, suggesting institutional conviction in the recovery trajectory [5].
thesis_continued: The bear case is that Under Armour's turnaround is now in its sixth year with no revenue growth in sight — FY2027 guidance calls for revenue 'down slightly' even after FY2025 (-9.4%) and FY2026 (-4%) contractions [1][2]. The $496M net loss in FY2026 (including $247M deferred tax valuation allowance) and -30% ROE reflect a business that is not yet earning its cost of capital. Nike (20.5% market share) and emerging brands like On Running and Hoka continue to take share from Under Armour's 2.3% position [3]. The $434M securities fraud settlement, while behind the company, consumed significant cash and management attention [6]. The Curry Brand exit removes a high-profile revenue stream without a clear replacement. Tariff exposure on Asian-sourced manufacturing could compress the gross margin recovery that is the centerpiece of the FY2027 bull case — management acknowledged $35M in Middle East conflict headwinds and significant IEEPA tariff impacts [2]. The forward P/E of 24.80 implies analysts expect profitability, but the company must execute flawlessly to justify even that modest premium, and Plank's self-described micromanagement style raises questions about organizational scalability [4].
key_catalysts: North American revenue stabilization in FY2027 H2 — the channel cleanup and off-price exit are largely complete, and management is investing $30M in incremental marketing to drive full-price demand; any evidence of flat or positive North American growth would validate the multi-year turnaround thesis

Gross margin expansion of 220-270 basis points in FY2027 materializing as guided, driven by mix shift to full-price distribution and operational efficiencies from the completed $305M restructuring, with 150bps from IEEPA tariff refunds providing additional near-term tailwind

EMEA and Latin America continuing to grow at high-single-digit rates, diversifying the revenue base away from the structurally challenged North American market and demonstrating that the Under Armour brand resonates globally in performance athletics
key_risks: Perpetual turnaround risk — Under Armour has been restructuring since 2019 with six consecutive years of strategic resets; investor patience for revenue contraction ('down slightly' in FY2027) is finite, and any execution miss could trigger further multiple compression from an already depressed $2.7B market cap

Tariff escalation on Asian-sourced manufacturing threatening the gross margin recovery that is the single most important financial metric in the turnaround story — management has already flagged $35M in Middle East conflict headwinds and significant IEEPA tariff exposure for FY2027

Competitive share erosion accelerating as Nike, On Running, Hoka, and Lululemon invest in AI-driven personalization, digital ecosystems, and brand storytelling at scales that Under Armour's 2.3% market share and constrained balance sheet cannot match
